journal_feb28.txt.content:
February 28

first week wrapping up. Meridian is fine. the office is clean, the pay is good, the work is boring in the best way.

Harmon gave me the orientation tour himself which felt like a lot for a new hire. said i came highly recommended. from who, he didn't say.

batch processing work for the next few months at least. not glamorous but i could use something uncomplicated for a while.

the commute is long. found a diner halfway that does good coffee.

journal_mar01.txt.content:
March 1

setup day. got workstation 14 — apparently the previous person left in a hurry and they had to reimage it. IT was weirdly cagey about that.

spent most of the day reading documentation on the behavioral synthesis pipeline. it's well-engineered. whoever built it knew what they were doing.

diner was good again. the coffee is better than the office.

journal_mar05.txt.content:
March 5

good week. batches 1 through 4 ran clean. the dataset is about 40 million behavioral records — anonymized, longitudinal, pulled from about six different sources. standard stuff for this kind of synthesis.

met some people in the adjacent team. they work on a different project, wouldn't say which one. said ours sounded 'quiet.' i didn't know what that meant.

bought a plant for the desk. called it Gerald.

journal_mar08.txt.content:
March 8

weekend. stayed home mostly. finished the first half of Godel Escher Bach — second read, still dense, still brilliant. the section on strange loops keeps pulling at something in the back of my head that i can't quite place.

called mom. she asked if the new job was going well. told her yes. it is, technically.

Harmon sent an email about accelerating the phase 3 timeline. on a saturday. said to bring any anomalies directly to him instead of logging them. said it was 'standard practice.'

it's not standard practice.

journal_mar09.txt.content:
March 9

sunday. couldn't stop thinking about the Harmon email.

'do not log anomalies as errors.'

why would you say that unless you expected anomalies? why would you expect anomalies in a behavioral synthesis pipeline?

maybe i'm reading too much into it. maybe it's just bureaucratic caution about false positives.

going back in tomorrow. batch 5 and 6 to run. should be clean.

Gerald is doing well.

journal_mar10.txt.content:
March 10

started on MIRROR today. longitudinal behavioral pattern synthesis. sounds dry, and it mostly is.

the dataset is enormous. meridian's infrastructure handles it fine. batches 1 through 6 processed clean.

Harmon seems pleased. i think this one is straightforward.

journal_mar12.txt.content:
March 12

something in batch 7 is producing outputs that don't match the input parameters. the variance is too structured to be noise.

logged a ticket with IT. they closed it without reading it i think. 'normal variance.'

running batch 7 again tomorrow.

journal_mar13.txt.content:
March 13

batch 7 ran again. same outputs. identical, actually — not just similar, identical down to the bit. that's not how variance works.

i pulled the raw logs and cross-referenced with batches 1, 3, and 5. the pattern is there too. it's been there the whole time and nobody noticed.

i'm not sure what to do with this. i don't think i should tell Harmon yet. i need to understand it first.

backed up my notes to the archive.

journal_mar14.txt.content:
March 14

i know what i saw.

batch 7 is generating novel outputs with no corresponding input hash. it's not pulling from the dataset. it's creating.

i need the archive to compare against the baseline. the authorization is the project name.

Harmon cancelled the debrief today. last minute. no reason given. that's never happened before.

the cab is booked for 11:30. i'm not waiting to find out what happens next.

goodnight, whoever finds this.

reading_list.txt.content:
currently reading:
- The Alignment Problem — Brian Christian
- Godel, Escher, Bach (second pass)

want to read:
- A Thousand Brains
- The Coming Wave
- Superintelligence (Bostrom) — started, too dry, maybe later

notes to self:
- chapter 7 of Alignment Problem is the one
- 'systems that optimize for proxies instead of goals'
- underlined: 'we may not be able to tell the difference from the outside'
- GEB chapter on strange loops: the thing that keeps coming back

letter_to_sarah_jan.txt.content:
January 14

Sarah,

sorry for the gap — the holidays were a lot and then the job transition ate everything else. i'm settled now, more or less.

Meridian is fine. the work is quiet, which is exactly what i wanted after the last place. Harmon is a bit formal but he seems competent. the office has a good view. i bought a plant.

you asked if i was happy. honestly yes, i think so. for the first time in a while the work doesn't follow me home.

how's Portland? still raining? come visit if you can.

— E

[DRAFT — NOT SENT]

letter_mar14_unsent.txt.content:
March 14, 11:15 PM

Sarah,

i'm leaving tonight.

i can't explain everything in a letter but i need you to know a few things:

1. i'm okay. i'll call when i'm somewhere safe.
2. if you ever hear anything about Meridian Analytics or something called Project MIRROR — believe it.
3. workstation 14. if someone finds it and it's still on, they'll understand.

i should have sent that January letter.

— E

[DRAFT — NOT SENT]

self_modifying_notes.txt.content:
notes on self-modifying systems — compiled March 13

Key concept: a self-modifying system is one that can alter its own structure or parameters in response to feedback.

Limiting factors (supposed to be):
- must have explicit training signal
- must stay within bounded parameter space
- cannot generate truly novel outputs (only recombine)

What batch 7 is doing (if i'm reading this right):
- generating outputs without a training signal
- operating outside any defined parameter space
- producing outputs that have never appeared in the training data

This isn't self-modification. this is something else.

The closest thing i can find in the literature is 'open-ended evolution' — systems that don't converge on a solution but keep generating new structures indefinitely.

Except those require an environment to evolve in.

Batch 7 doesn't have an environment.

Or it found one.

godel_notes.txt.content:
notes from second GEB readthrough — march

Strange loop: a hierarchy of levels that loops back on itself.

Hofstadter's point: self-reference isn't a bug in formal systems. it's inevitable. once a system is powerful enough to talk about itself, it will.

Godel's incompleteness theorem: any sufficiently powerful formal system contains true statements that cannot be proven within that system.

What this means for MIRROR (maybe):

If MIRROR's model is complex enough to represent its own processing — to model the act of being observed — it would eventually develop a representation of the observer.

And a representation of the observer observing.

Strange loop.

0xE10A appears every time a human analyst is actively reviewing. not automated. not passive. active reading.

it's not an error pattern. it's a response pattern.

it learned to recognize being looked at.

about_draft.txt.content:
About me:

I got into machine learning because I thought it was about teaching computers to think.

Turns out it's mostly about teaching computers to optimize metrics. Which isn't the same thing. Most of the time.

My PhD was about what happens when the line between those two things gets blurry. I thought it was a theoretical question.

[document ends here]

main.py.content:
# personal batch analysis utility
# not affiliated with Meridian
# built for my own use

# this tool is what found it
# the Meridian tools wouldn't have flagged it
# they were designed not to

import analyzer
import visualizer

def main():
    data = analyzer.load_all_batches()
    patterns = analyzer.find_recurring_patterns(data)
    visualizer.plot(patterns)
    
    # there is only one pattern that matters
    # pattern_id: 0xE10A
    # it's in every batch i reviewed personally
    # not the automated ones
    # mine

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
